my_sentence: I think maybe more than two hundred millions.
correction: I think maybe more than two hundred million.
explanation: When 'million' / 'billion' follows an exact number (two hundred, five, etc.), it stays singular: 'two hundred million', not 'two hundred millions'. You only add -s in the vague plural form 'millions of ...'.
correct_examples: China alone has about two hundred million mobile game users.
The film earned more than five billion yuan.
context: Estimating the number of mobile game users in China.

my_sentence: They will charge more than hundreds of billion yuan.
correction: They can charge hundreds of billions of yuan.
explanation: In the vague-quantity pattern the noun is plural and takes 'of': 'hundreds of billions of yuan'. Compare with rule for exact numbers ('two hundred million'): after 'hundreds of', use plural 'billions of'.
correct_examples: The industry generates hundreds of billions of yuan every year.
They spend thousands of dollars on games.
context: How much money mobile games generate yearly in China.

my_sentence: Tencent company in my country is the biggest.
correction: Tencent Company is the biggest in my country.
explanation: Keep the subject and its verb together, and put the place phrase after the complement: 'Tencent is the biggest [company] in my country'. Don't split the subject with 'in my country'.
correct_examples: Tencent is the biggest company in my country.
This is the tallest building in the city.
context: Saying which gaming company is the biggest in China.

my_sentence: I don't play computer games, so I don't know something about them.
correction: I don't play computer games, so I don't know anything about them.
explanation: In a negative sentence use 'anything', not 'something'. 'I don't know something' is a Chinese-influenced pattern; the negative polarity word is 'anything'. (assistant note)
correct_examples: I don't know anything about it.
She didn't say anything about the plan.
context: Saying he doesn't know the game Gameloft.

my_sentence: You don't want to go to another places just to watch a movie.
correction: You don't want to go to another place just to watch a movie.
explanation: 'another' is followed by a singular noun ('another place'); for a plural noun use 'other' ('other places'). The teacher stressed: another + singular, other + plural.
correct_examples: Let's try another place next time.
There are many other places worth visiting.
context: Why people prefer watching movies at home rather than travelling to a cinema.

my_sentence: If you want to build a tent, it's not suitable when is raining.
correction: It's not suitable if it's raining.
explanation: A weather clause needs the dummy subject 'it': 'when/if it's raining', not 'when is raining'. English clauses can't drop the subject the way Chinese does.
correct_examples: We can't go out because it's raining.
It's not a good idea to camp when it's raining.
context: Explaining why you can't camp outdoors on a rainy day.

my_sentence: I'm all enjoy the winter day.
correction: I enjoy winter more.
explanation: Don't put 'be' before an ordinary verb: use the plain present 'I enjoy', not 'I'm ... enjoy'. Also, the season is just 'winter' (no 'day'), and for a preference use 'more'.
correct_examples: I enjoy winter more than summer.
I really enjoy reading.
context: Saying which season he likes best.

my_sentence: There are many snow in my city.
correction: There is a lot of snow in my city.
explanation: 'snow' is uncountable, so it takes singular 'is' and the quantifier 'a lot of' / 'much', not 'many'. 'many' only goes with countable plural nouns.
correct_examples: There is a lot of snow in winter.
We don't get much rain here.
context: Explaining why he enjoys winter.

my_sentence: Most of them don't have English name.
correction: Most of them don't have English names.
explanation: When several films each have a name, use the plural 'names'. A countable noun referring to more than one thing should be plural.
correct_examples: These movies don't have English names.
The students wrote their names on the sheet.
context: Chinese animated movies not being known abroad.

my_sentence: I think I didn't heard of it.
correction: I didn't hear of it. / I haven't heard of it.
explanation: After the auxiliary 'did/didn't' the main verb stays in the base form: 'didn't hear', not 'didn't heard'. To stress it's news up to now, 'I haven't heard of it' is even more natural.
correct_examples: I didn't hear about the meeting.
I haven't heard of that movie.
context: Saying he had never heard of the anime 'Legend of Aang'.

my_sentence: There are too much poor people, they don't have money to watch movies.
correction: There are too many poor people, so they don't have money to watch movies.
explanation: 'people' is countable plural, so use 'too many', not 'too much' (which is for uncountable nouns). (assistant note)
correct_examples: There are too many people in this room.
I have too much work today.
context: Explaining why many Indians don't spend money on movies.

my_sentence: Their films are not enough good.
correction: Their films are not good enough.
explanation: 'enough' comes AFTER an adjective: 'good enough', not 'enough good'. (It comes before a noun: 'enough money'.)
correct_examples: This film isn't good enough to win a prize.
The room isn't big enough for everyone.
context: Saying Hong Kong films are no longer good enough.

my_sentence: Nowadays their films become worse and worse.
correction: Nowadays their films are becoming worse and worse.
explanation: For a change happening over time right now, use the present continuous: 'are becoming worse and worse'. The '... and ...' comparative pattern describes an ongoing trend.
correct_examples: The weather is getting colder and colder.
The city is becoming more and more crowded.
context: Describing the decline of Hong Kong cinema.

my_sentence: There are too many companies that built in China.
correction: There are too many companies that are being built in China.
explanation: The companies are built by someone, so this needs the passive: 'that are being built'. 'that built' is active and would mean the companies did the building.
correct_examples: Many new factories are being built in the area.
These houses were built ten years ago.
context: Explaining why Hong Kong studios now face many competitors.

my_sentence: For me, I'm more prefer to earn more money, not become famous.
correction: I prefer to earn more money, not to become famous.
explanation: 'prefer' is a normal verb: say 'I prefer', not 'I'm prefer' (no 'be', no 'more'). Keep the two options parallel with 'to': 'to earn money, not to become famous'.
correct_examples: I prefer to stay home rather than go out.
She chose to study, not to travel.
context: Saying he'd rather make money than be famous.

my_sentence: You need to join private classes to improve, and improve, and improve.
correction: You need to join private classes to make progress.
explanation: Don't repeat 'improve' over and over; vary it with 'make progress'. Note the collocation is 'make progress' (uncountable, no article) — not 'have a progress'.
correct_examples: Extra classes help students make progress.
She has made a lot of progress this year.
context: Talking about private tutoring to raise children's scores.

my_sentence: I think it's very similar from other countries.
correction: I think it's very similar to schools in other countries.
explanation: 'similar' takes 'to', not 'from': 'similar to ...'. (assistant note)
correct_examples: This system is similar to the one in Japan.
Her opinion is similar to mine.
context: Comparing international schools in China with schools abroad.

my_sentence: If their classes have more than 50 students, their tuition fee will very low.
correction: If their classes have more than 50 students, their tuition fee will be very low.
explanation: 'will' must be followed by a base verb; before an adjective you need 'be': 'will be very low'. You can't link a subject to an adjective without a verb.
correct_examples: The price will be very high next year.
If it rains, the road will be slippery.
context: How class size affects tuition fees.

my_sentence: This money need to pay by their parents.
correction: This money needs to be paid by their parents.
explanation: Two fixes: 'money' is singular, so 'needs' (agreement); and the money is paid by someone, so the passive infinitive 'to be paid' is required, not the active 'to pay'.
correct_examples: This bill needs to be paid by Friday.
The form needs to be signed by a parent.
context: Who pays international-school tuition fees.

my_sentence: The government will pay zero for these schools.
correction: The government will not pay any amount of money to these schools.
explanation: 'pay zero for' sounds unnatural. Use 'not pay any money to ...' (or simply 'pays nothing toward these schools'). Also note you 'pay money TO' an institution.
correct_examples: The government doesn't pay any money to private schools.
They paid nothing toward the repairs.
context: Saying the government funds nothing for international schools.

my_sentence: I mentioned that before; I find their tuition fee is so high.
correction: I found that their tuition fee was so high.
explanation: Reporting something you said before takes the past tense, and the reported clause backshifts too: 'I found that their tuition fee was so high.'
correct_examples: I found that the course was very useful.
She said the price was too high.
context: Recalling an earlier comment about high university tuition abroad.
